content:
37 min read
Just now

--

Part 1 built the blueprint: the demand inventory, the legacy table triage, the entity mapping, and the layer-stamped source-to-target map that divides all logic between the database model and the Power BI model. Part 2 now builds everything stamped L1–L3: the layered pipeline, the dimensions, the facts, the keys and history, the loading patterns, and the reconciliation harness that makes parity provable. Part 3 will build the semantic model and run the migration to cutover.

The database model is the load-bearing wall of the migration. Every measure Part 3 writes, every report page rebuilt, every reconciliation run, and every performance characteristic users experience sits on the tables designed here. That sounds like pressure; it should sound like the opposite. Because Part 1 already made the hard decisions — what’s in scope, what collapses into what, which logic lands here — Part 2 is largely the disciplined execution of standard patterns. Dimensional modeling is a mature craft: for essentially every situation a migration produces, there is a boring, documented, battle-tested pattern. This article is the complete set of those patterns, arranged in build order, with the migration-specific judgment calls highlighted at each step.
A vocabulary note before starting: this article uses the medallion naming (raw/bronze → silver → gold) because it’s currently the most widespread, but every pattern here maps directly onto staging/integration/presentation, landing/core/mart, or whatever your platform’s culture calls its layers. The layer responsibilities are the invariant; the names are fashion.
Section 1: The Layers in Practice — What Each One Owes the Next
Part 1 defined the five logic homes abstractly. Building them means giving each layer a precise service contract with the layer above it, because “clean” and “integrated” are opinions until written as checkable promises.
1.1 Raw/Landing (L1): the unarguable copy
The contract: every source extract lands exactly as received — same columns, same values, same mess — plus load metadata (load timestamp, batch/run identifier, source system tag, source file name where applicable). Nothing is ever updated in place; new extracts append or land as new snapshots.
Why the fundamentalism about “no transformation, not even trimming”? Three migration-specific reasons. First, replay: silver and gold logic will have bugs discovered weeks later; with faithful raw data, the fix is “correct the transformation, rebuild downstream” rather than “re-extract from a legacy system that may no longer exist by then.” In a migration, raw is your time machine, and the legacy system’s decommission date is exactly why you need one. Second, arbitration: when reconciliation disputes arise (“the old report says X, your model says Y”), raw data is the neutral evidence of what the source actually delivered — no one can claim your pipeline altered it. Third, the Bucket E promise: Part 1’s triage archived a third of the legacy estate as “kept, not modeled”; L1 is where that promise physically lives, cheaply.
Design decisions to make once, globally: the raw naming convention (raw.<source_system>__<object> works well — the double underscore keeps source and object parseable); the snapshot strategy per source (append-only change feeds vs. periodic full snapshots — full snapshots are the safe default for smaller reference tables, change-based for large facts); and retention (keep everything for the migration's duration plus a comfortable audit window; storage is the cheapest thing in the entire project).
1.2 Silver/Staging (L2): trustworthy, still source-shaped
The contract: one silver table per meaningful source object, same grain and shape as the source, but now: correctly typed, deduplicated by business key, standardized (trim/case/null semantics/code normalization/timezone), validated with failures quarantined, and soft-delete semantics decoded. The shape still mirrors the source — silver does not join across tables or apply business meaning. Its one-sentence job: make each source’s data safe to build on, without yet deciding what to build.
The silver rules that repay their discipline, each stamped from Part 1’s placement matrix:
Typing with quarantine, not coercion. Every column gets its declared type, and rows that fail conversion go to a quarantine table with the failure reason — they do not become NULLs silently, because silent coercion is how reconciliation ends up chasing 0.3% discrepancies for a week. The quarantine table is itself part of the reconciliation math: raw count = silver count + quarantined count, checkable automatically.
Deduplication with a written survivorship rule. Legacy extracts duplicate rows constantly (rerun batches, CDC replays, genuine source dupes). Silver dedups by the business key with an explicit winner rule — usually “latest by source timestamp wins,” sometimes source-precedence — recorded on the STM rows. The dedup counts get logged per run; a sudden spike in duplicates is an upstream-change alarm you want ringing early.
Standardization as a single shared library. Trimming, case rules, null-vs-empty-vs-sentinel semantics ('N/A', '-', 0001-01-01 — every legacy system has its favorite fake nulls; find them in profiling, map them to real NULLs here), boolean normalization, and timezone normalization to one declared standard. Implement these as shared, tested functions/macros applied uniformly — twenty hand-written variations of "clean a code column" is how the same source value becomes three different silver values in three tables.
Soft-delete decoding. Legacy systems mark deletion in wonderful ways: status flags, delete-date columns, tombstone rows, or the row silently vanishing between snapshots. Silver converts whatever the convention is into an explicit, uniform is_deleted flag (and for vanishing-row sources, a snapshot-comparison step to detect the vanishing). Gold then decides per entity whether deleted rows are excluded or kept-and-flagged — a business decision from the entity sheets, not a pipeline default.
What silver explicitly does not do, because the placement matrix said so: no cross-source joins, no surrogate keys, no SCD, no business flags, no grain changes. When you feel the urge to “just join the region lookup in while we’re here” — that’s gold work leaking upstream, and the leak’s cost is that silver stops being independently reconcilable against its single source.
1.3 Gold (L3): the stars
The contract: presentation-ready star schemas — conformed dimensions and declared-grain facts — plus the small set of supporting tables (security mappings, agg tables when justified). Every table’s grain documented; every business rule from the STM implemented exactly once; unknown-member conventions applied; loads idempotent and incremental. This is the layer the semantic model consumes as-is, so its shape is the contract with Part 3.
The rest of this article lives in gold: Section 2 builds the dimensions, Section 3 the facts, Section 4 the loading machinery, Section 5 the testing and reconciliation harness, and Section 6 the assembly workflow that sequences it all without derailing.
One structural decision before diving in: schema organization. Keep gold in one clearly named schema (or one per domain with conformed dimensions in a shared gold_common), with the iron rule that the semantic model reads only from gold. The moment a "temporary" silver table gets exposed to Power BI because it was handy, the contract has a hole, and holes only widen. If a slice genuinely needs data before its gold table is ready, build a deliberately minimal gold table (even a thin view over silver, named and documented as an interim gold contract with a replacement ticket) — the discipline isn't about ceremony, it's about the semantic model never needing to know which layer politics produced its tables.
Section 2: Dimension Design — The Complete Playbook
Dimensions carry most of gold’s design content. This section walks the full standard toolkit in the order you’ll need it, with the migration judgment calls marked.
2.1 The anatomy of a well-built dimension
Every dimension, regardless of subject, has the same skeleton:

• Surrogate key — a warehouse-generated identifier (sequential integer or deterministic hash of business key + validity; pick one style per platform and never mix). It is the only column facts reference.

• Business/natural key(s) — the source system’s identifier(s), kept for lineage, matching, and reconciliation. With multiple sources (Part 1’s Pattern 2), keep each source’s key in its own column plus a unified key where matching produced one.

• Attributes — the descriptive payload, flattened and wide: names, categories, hierarchies-as-columns, geography, types, statuses, and the dissolved content of every code lookup that described this entity. Wide is a feature; a fifty-column dimension is normal and cheap, because dimensions are small.

• SCD housekeeping (where Type 2 applies): valid_from, valid_to, is_current.

• Audit columns: load timestamp, batch id, source-of-record tag per row.
And two universal rows/conventions:
The unknown member. Every dimension contains a row with surrogate key -1 (or 0, pick once) representing "Unknown / not provided," and often a -2 for "Not applicable" where the distinction matters to the business. Fact loads map unmatched keys to -1 rather than dropping rows or storing NULL keys. This single convention converts the most common class of silent reconciliation failure (rows lost to failed joins) into a visible, measurable line on every report — "Unknown: 1.4%" — which is simultaneously a data-quality dashboard and a guarantee that totals still reconcile. Beginners sometimes resist "polluting" dimensions with fake rows; the day the first fact load meets a customer code that master data hasn't seen yet, the resistance ends.
Attribute naming for humans. Gold dimension columns are named in business language (customer_segment, not SEG_CD_03), because per the contract Part 3 consumes them nearly as-is. The STM already holds the legacy-to-business rename map from the reverse engineering; gold is where it gets applied, once, for every downstream consumer.
2.2 The Date dimension: build it first, build it rich
Every model needs exactly one physical date dimension, one row per calendar day, spanning from the earliest fact date to a few years beyond today. Generate it from a script (never type it, never trust a legacy calendar table’s shape — though harvest its business content per Part 1’s Pattern 5). The column set that earns its keep:

• Keys and basics: date_key (the pragmatic yyyymmdd integer), date, year, quarter, month number/name/short-name, week (with the week-numbering standard your business actually uses — ISO vs. US matters and differs), day of week/month/year.

• Fiscal columns as data: fiscal year, fiscal quarter, fiscal period, fiscal week, with the organization’s real year-end and any 4–4–5 or custom period logic encoded here — not in DAX. Part 3’s time intelligence becomes trivial when the calendar’s weirdness is just columns.

• Flags: is_working_day (with the holiday calendar loaded as data, per region if needed), is_month_end, is_fiscal_period_end, is_closed (accounting close status, if reporting distinguishes closed vs. open periods — harvest from those legacy control tables).

• Relative-date helpers where reports use them: offsets like months_ago, is_current_month — refreshed daily, they make "current period" report filters robust without DAX gymnastics. (Purists note these make the table non-static; the load handles it — see Section 4.)
If any fact needs time-of-day analysis, add a separate small dim_time (one row per minute or second of day) rather than datetime keys on facts — Part 3's performance section will thank you, since datetime columns are cardinality bombs in columnar engines.
2.3 History: the SCD decision, per attribute, with a migration twist
